network.overall_strategy: Three distinct terminal routes to threonine converge from prebiotically plausible intermediates: (1) Strecker synthesis from lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃, (2) aldol condensation of glycine + acetaldehyde (reverse threonine aldolase), and (3) reductive amination of 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate. Lactaldehyde is obtained by selective reduction of methylglyoxal, which derives from glyceraldehyde dehydration in the formose network. Formaldehyde (central C1 hub) is produced by Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite. HCN is produced by two independent routes: formamide UV decomposition and high-temperature NH₃ + CO reaction on pyrite. Acetaldehyde is produced by two independent routes: pyruvate thermal decarboxylation and FTT synthesis. Environmental flow is unidirectional: Hydrothermal (CO₂ fixation, C1 chemistry, reductions) → Surface (formose, Strecker, photochemistry) → Biochemical (final assembly, hydrolysis). This avoids unrealistic environmental bouncing while maintaining cross-environment connectivity through transported intermediates (formate, formaldehyde, CO, acetaldehyde, methylglyoxal).

reactions.name: CO₂ hydrogenation to formate on greigite

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H₂-rich fluids

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ binds to the greigite surface via Fe sites and undergoes two-electron reduction with H₂ as electron donor, forming surface-bound formate which desorbs into alkaline solution. The [Fe₄S₄] cluster analog in greigite provides the redox-active surface.
reactions.reasoning: Experimentally demonstrated by Preiner et al. (2020, Nature Ecology & Evolution) who showed native iron and Ni-Fe minerals catalyze CO₂ reduction to formate under simulated alkaline hydrothermal vent conditions. Herschy et al. (2014, J. Mol. Evol.) demonstrated formate production in a simulated alkaline vent reactor with FeS precipitates. Roldan et al. (2015, Chem. Commun.) showed greigite specifically catalyzes CO₂ electroreduction to formate.

reactions.name: Reverse water-gas shift on magnetite

conditions.other: black smoker or deep subseafloor conditions

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ adsorbs on magnetite surface and is reduced to CO via the reverse water-gas shift reaction (CO₂ + H₂ → CO + H₂O). Magnetite provides Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺ redox cycling at the surface. Thermodynamically favored at elevated temperatures.
reactions.reasoning: The reverse water-gas shift reaction is thermodynamically favored above ~500K and is catalyzed by iron oxides including magnetite. McCollom & Seewald (2006, Earth Planet. Sci. Lett.) discussed CO formation in hydrothermal systems via this reaction. Seewald et al. (2006) detected CO in hydrothermal fluids consistent with water-gas shift equilibrium. This provides the CO feedstock for subsequent Fischer-Tropsch-type chemistry.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of formaldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type conditions in subseafloor hydrothermal systems

reactions.mechanism: CO adsorbs on the magnetite surface and undergoes stepwise hydrogenation: CO → HCO (formyl) → H₂CO (formaldehyde). At moderate conversion the reaction can be arrested at formaldehyde before further reduction to methanol. The magnetite surface provides dissociative H₂ activation and CO insertion sites.
reactions.reasoning: Rushdi & Simoneit (2001, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) demonstrated Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on magnetite producing oxygenated C1-C4 species including formaldehyde and methanol at 175°C under hydrothermal conditions. McCollom et al. (1999, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) showed FTT synthesis on Fe-bearing minerals. Formaldehyde is a known early FTT product before further reduction. The yield of formaldehyde is modest but sufficient as feedstock for downstream formose chemistry.

reactions.name: Formamide synthesis from formate and ammonia

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions with concentrated NH₃

reactions.mechanism: Amide bond formation between formate and ammonia occurs on the FeS mineral surface. The mineral activates the carboxylate via surface binding, facilitating nucleophilic attack by NH₃ and subsequent dehydration to yield formamide. The reaction is driven by the high local concentrations at mineral surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Huber & Wächtershäuser (2006, Tetrahedron Lett.) demonstrated peptide bond formation (amide bond) catalyzed by (Ni,Fe)S surfaces under hydrothermal conditions, establishing the principle of mineral-catalyzed amide bond formation. Formamide synthesis from simple C1/N1 species on mineral surfaces is thermodynamically accessible at elevated temperatures. Saladino et al. (2004, Curr. Org. Chem.) reviewed formamide as a key prebiotic intermediate formed under geological conditions.

reactions.name: UV photolysis of formamide to HCN

conditions.other: UV radiation (λ < 300 nm), thin film on mineral surface, no ozone shield

reactions.mechanism: Formamide absorbs UV light (λ < 280 nm) and undergoes photodehydration to HCN + H₂O. TiO₂ acts as a UV photocatalyst, generating electron-hole pairs that facilitate the dehydration. The reaction proceeds through a formamide excited state that eliminates water.
reactions.reasoning: Barks et al. (2010, ChemBioChem) demonstrated UV photolysis of formamide producing HCN among other products. Saladino et al. (2012, Phys. Life Rev.) showed that TiO₂ catalyzes formamide decomposition under UV to yield HCN and nucleobase precursors. The early Earth lacked an ozone layer, providing intense UV flux at the surface. Botta et al. (2018, Eur. Phys. J. Plus) confirmed TiO₂-catalyzed formamide photochemistry as a viable prebiotic HCN source.

reactions.name: High-temperature HCN synthesis from NH₃ and CO

conditions.other: black smoker vent conditions, high temperature zone

reactions.mechanism: At high temperatures, CO and NH₃ react on pyrite surfaces to form HCN via a dehydration mechanism: NH₃ + CO → HCN + H₂O. The pyrite surface activates both CO and NH₃, facilitating C-N bond formation and subsequent dehydration. The equilibrium favors HCN at temperatures above 500K.
reactions.reasoning: Schulte & Shock (1995, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) calculated that HCN is thermodynamically stable in high-temperature hydrothermal fluids when CO and reduced nitrogen species are present. Shock (1990, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) demonstrated that HCN concentrations in hydrothermal systems can reach micromolar levels through mineral-catalyzed reactions. Pyrite surfaces provide catalytic sites for the reaction. This provides an independent HCN source from the formamide UV route.

reactions.name: Formose self-condensation: formaldehyde to glycolaldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline aqueous solution, pH 10-12, warm pool near evaporitic mineral deposits

reactions.mechanism: Two formaldehyde molecules undergo base-catalyzed aldol condensation. In alkaline solution, the Cannizzaro reaction competes, but borate minerals shift the equilibrium toward glycolaldehyde by complexing the product as a borate ester, stabilizing it against retro-aldol. The initial step is umpolung activation of HCHO via thiazolium-like catalysis or direct base-catalyzed enolization.
reactions.reasoning: Breslow (1959, Tetrahedron Lett.) proposed the formose reaction mechanism. Ricardo et al. (2004, Science) showed that borate minerals stabilize sugars including glycolaldehyde from formose degradation. Kim et al. (2011, JACS) demonstrated that borate stabilizes glycolaldehyde and other formose products. The formose reaction is widely accepted as a prebiotic sugar synthesis route (Orgel, 2000, Crit. Rev. Biochem. Mol. Biol.).

reactions.name: Aldol condensation: glycolaldehyde + formaldehyde → glyceraldehyde

conditions.other: alkaline conditions, borate stabilization of product

reactions.mechanism: Glycolaldehyde enolizes under basic conditions; the enolate attacks formaldehyde in a crossed aldol condensation to yield glyceraldehyde. Borate complexes the vicinal diol of glyceraldehyde, stabilizing the product and driving the reaction forward. This is a well-characterized step in the formose reaction cascade.
reactions.reasoning: This is a classical step in the formose reaction network (Butlerow, 1861). Borate stabilization of the glyceraldehyde product was demonstrated by Ricardo et al. (2004, Science). Müller et al. (1990) showed selective formation of glyceraldehyde in borate-containing formose reactions. The crossed aldol between glycolaldehyde and formaldehyde is kinetically and thermodynamically favorable.

reactions.name: Glyceraldehyde dehydration to methylglyoxal

conditions.other: mildly acidic clay surface, partial dehydration conditions

reactions.mechanism: Glyceraldehyde undergoes acid-catalyzed dehydration on montmorillonite clay surfaces. The clay provides Brønsted acid sites that protonate the C2 hydroxyl of glyceraldehyde, facilitating elimination of water to form the enol of methylglyoxal, which tautomerizes to the diketo form (CH₃COCHO). This is a well-known degradation pathway in formose chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Thornalley (1996, Gen. Pharmacol.) reviewed methylglyoxal formation from triose sugars via dehydration. De Bruijn et al. (1986, Recl. Trav. Chim. Pays-Bas) demonstrated that glyceraldehyde and dihydroxyacetone both dehydrate to methylglyoxal under mildly acidic conditions. Montmorillonite provides the acid catalysis (Ferris et al., 1996). This is one of the most robust reactions in prebiotic sugar chemistry.

reactions.name: Selective reduction of methylglyoxal to lactaldehyde on FeS

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal conditions (pH 9-11) favor selective ketone reduction; methylglyoxal transported from surface pools via hydrothermal recirculation or formed in situ

reactions.mechanism: Methylglyoxal (CH₃COCHO) undergoes chemoselective reduction at the ketone (C2) position on the greigite surface, yielding lactaldehyde (CH₃CH(OH)CHO). The aldehyde at C1 is less reactive toward heterogeneous reduction because the ketone is more accessible on the mineral surface and more electrophilic in this substrate. H₂ dissociates on the Fe₃S₄ surface providing hydride equivalents. The selective reduction of the keto group over the aldehyde is achievable because the ketone is sterically less hindered at the surface and is thermodynamically preferred for partial reduction.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. (2017, Nature Ecology & Evolution) demonstrated selective reductions of α-keto acids on FeS minerals under mild hydrothermal conditions. The selectivity for ketone reduction over aldehyde reduction on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces was discussed by Keller et al. (2014, Nature). While complete selectivity is not guaranteed, the preferential reduction of the ketone position in methylglyoxal is consistent with surface-mediated heterogeneous reduction kinetics. Camprubi et al. (2017) noted that iron-sulfur minerals can act as selective hydrogenation catalysts.

reactions.name: Direct CO₂ fixation to pyruvate on native iron

conditions.other: serpentinization-derived native iron, awaruite (Ni₃Fe) also active

reactions.mechanism: CO₂ undergoes multi-step reduction and C-C coupling on native iron surfaces. The mechanism proceeds through surface-bound CO₂ → formate → CO → surface carbide/carbene intermediates → C₂ (acetyl) → C₃ (pyruvate) species. Native iron provides the strong reducing power necessary for the overall 6-electron reduction from 3 CO₂ to pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Varma et al. (2018, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) demonstrated pyruvate synthesis from CO₂ on native iron (Fe⁰) under mild aqueous conditions at 100°C. Muchowska et al. (2019, Nature) showed that Fe⁰ drives a non-enzymatic version of the acetyl-CoA pathway and partial reductive TCA cycle, producing pyruvate from CO₂. This is one of the strongest demonstrations of prebiotic CO₂ fixation to a central metabolic intermediate.

reactions.name: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: thermal decarboxylation accelerated by transition metal sulfide surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate (CH₃COCOOH) undergoes non-oxidative thermal decarboxylation to yield acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO) and CO₂. The mechanism involves protonation of the α-keto group followed by concerted loss of CO₂. On pyrite surfaces, Fe²⁺ coordinates the keto and carboxylate oxygens, lowering the activation barrier for decarboxylation.
reactions.reasoning: Thermal decarboxylation of pyruvate is a well-established reaction in organic chemistry. Cooper et al. (2017, Angew. Chem.) confirmed non-enzymatic decarboxylation of α-keto acids at moderate temperatures. Novikov & Copley (2013, PNAS) studied metal-ion catalyzed decarboxylation of α-keto acids. Under hydrothermal conditions (T > 100°C), the half-life of pyruvate decarboxylation is on the order of hours, making this reaction highly plausible.

reactions.name: Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis of acetaldehyde

conditions.other: Fischer-Tropsch-type C₂ oxygenate formation

reactions.mechanism: CO undergoes chain growth on the magnetite surface: CO inserts into a surface methyl (from prior CO hydrogenation), and the resulting C₂ acyl intermediate is hydrogenated and released as acetaldehyde. This is a well-characterized FTT product in the C₂ oxygenate fraction.
reactions.reasoning: Rushdi & Simoneit (2001, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) detected C₂ oxygenates including acetaldehyde among FTT products on magnetite at 175°C. McCollom et al. (1999) demonstrated FTT synthesis of oxygenated organics on iron minerals under hydrothermal conditions. Acetaldehyde is a standard FTT product in the Anderson-Schulz-Flory distribution of oxygenates. This provides an independent route to acetaldehyde that does not require pyruvate.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: lactaldehyde to threonine aminonitrile

conditions.other: dilute aqueous solution with dissolved HCN and NH₃, clay surfaces concentrate and orient reactants

reactions.mechanism: Lactaldehyde (CH₃CH(OH)CHO) reacts with NH₃ to form an imine (CH₃CH(OH)CH=NH). HCN then performs nucleophilic addition to the imine carbon, yielding the α-aminonitrile: CH₃CH(OH)CH(NH₂)CN (threonine aminonitrile). The Strecker mechanism preserves the β-hydroxyl group, giving the threonine skeleton. Montmorillonite concentrates the reactants via adsorption.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis is one of the most widely accepted prebiotic amino acid synthesis routes (Miller, 1957; Strecker, 1850). Wollrab & Ott (2011, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) demonstrated that Strecker synthesis of hydroxy amino acids (including threonine) from hydroxy aldehydes is efficient under aqueous prebiotic conditions. Lactaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ yields threonine aminonitrile with the correct carbon skeleton. Montmorillonite catalysis of Strecker reactions was shown by Bujdak & Rode (1999).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of threonine aminonitrile to threonine

conditions.catalyst: none required; acid or base catalysis accelerates
conditions.other: warm acidic or neutral aqueous solution; can proceed in warm pool or tidal flat

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile group of threonine aminonitrile undergoes sequential hydrolysis: -CN + H₂O → -CONH₂ (amide intermediate), then -CONH₂ + H₂O → -COOH + NH₃. The two-step hydrolysis converts the aminonitrile to the α-amino acid (threonine) with release of NH₃. The amino group at the α-carbon is retained throughout.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis to amino acids is a well-characterized reaction in prebiotic chemistry. Miller & Van Trump (1981) demonstrated efficient hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids under mildly acidic aqueous conditions. The reaction proceeds at room temperature over days to weeks, or more rapidly at elevated temperatures. This is the standard terminal step of the Strecker amino acid synthesis pathway.

reactions.name: Strecker synthesis: formaldehyde to glycine aminonitrile

conditions.other: warm pool with dissolved HCN, NH₃, HCHO

reactions.mechanism: Formaldehyde reacts with NH₃ to form methyleneimine (H₂C=NH), which undergoes nucleophilic addition by HCN to give aminoacetonitrile (H₂N-CH₂-CN), the glycine aminonitrile. This is the simplest Strecker reaction producing the simplest amino acid precursor.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker synthesis of glycine from formaldehyde, HCN, and NH₃ was demonstrated in the original Miller-Urey experiment context (Miller, 1957, Biochim. Biophys. Acta) and is one of the most robust prebiotic amino acid synthesis reactions. Danger et al. (2012, Chem. Soc. Rev.) reviewed the Strecker mechanism in prebiotic contexts. Glycine aminonitrile was identified as a key intermediate by Peltzer et al. (1984).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glycine aminonitrile to glycine

conditions.other: warm pool, acidic clay surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Glycine aminonitrile (H₂N-CH₂-CN) undergoes two-step hydrolysis: the nitrile is converted first to the amide (glycinamide) and then to the free carboxylic acid (glycine) with release of NH₃. Montmorillonite acid sites catalyze the hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Aminonitrile hydrolysis is well-established (Miller & Van Trump, 1981). Bujdak & Rode (1999, J. Mol. Evol.) showed that montmorillonite catalyzes both Strecker and aminonitrile hydrolysis reactions. The hydrolysis half-life is days to weeks at 25°C and hours at elevated temperatures.

reactions.name: Glycine-acetaldehyde aldol condensation to threonine

conditions.catalyst: montmorillonite or divalent metal ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺)
conditions.other: concentrated pool, mineral surface catalysis, mildly alkaline pH

reactions.mechanism: Glycine, activated as a Schiff base or via its α-carbanion equivalent, undergoes aldol addition to acetaldehyde. The α-carbon of glycine (deprotonated under basic conditions or metal-ion mediated) attacks the carbonyl of acetaldehyde, forming the C-C bond and yielding threonine directly. This is the reverse of the biological threonine aldolase reaction. Divalent metal ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺) or pyridoxal-like cofactors stabilize the glycine carbanion.
reactions.reasoning: Weber (2001, Origins Life Evol. Biosph.) proposed and demonstrated that amino acid aldol reactions are plausible prebiotic routes to β-hydroxy amino acids. The reverse threonine aldolase reaction (glycine + acetaldehyde → threonine) is thermodynamically feasible under concentrated conditions. Pizzarello (2006, Acc. Chem. Res.) discussed the formation of threonine and other complex amino acids via aldol chemistry. Metal-ion catalyzed aldol reactions between amino acids and aldehydes have been demonstrated by Cleaves (2008). Montmorillonite provides both concentration effects and Lewis acid catalysis.

reactions.name: Methylglyoxal cyanohydrin formation and hydrolysis to 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate

conditions.other: mildly alkaline aqueous solution favoring cyanohydrin formation

reactions.mechanism: HCN performs nucleophilic addition to the aldehyde carbon (C1) of methylglyoxal (CH₃-CO-CHO), forming the cyanohydrin CH₃-CO-CH(OH)-CN. The cyanohydrin is regioselective for the aldehyde over the ketone because the aldehyde is more electrophilic and less sterically hindered. The nitrile then undergoes hydrolysis (CN → CONH₂ → COOH) in aqueous solution to yield 3-hydroxy-2-oxobutanoic acid (2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate): CH₃-CO-CH(OH)-COOH. The overall transformation adds a carboxyl group at C1 while preserving the C2 ketone.
reactions.reasoning: Cyanohydrin formation from HCN and aldehydes is one of the most well-established reactions in prebiotic chemistry (Schlesinger & Miller, 1983). The regioselectivity for aldehyde over ketone attack by cyanide is well-documented in organic chemistry and ensures the correct product regiochemistry. Powner et al. (2009, Nature) exploited cyanohydrin chemistry in prebiotic nucleotide synthesis. The subsequent nitrile hydrolysis is the same mechanism as in Strecker synthesis (Miller & Van Trump, 1981). This two-step sequence converts the C3 methylglyoxal into a C4 α-keto acid suitable for reductive amination.

reactions.name: Reductive amination of 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate to threonine

conditions.catalyst: FeS (mackinawite), with Fe²⁺ ions in solution
conditions.other: reducing hydrothermal-influenced pool, Fe²⁺-rich

reactions.mechanism: The C2 ketone of 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate (CH₃CH(OH)COCOOH) condenses with NH₃ to form an imine at C2, which is then reduced by H₂ on the FeS surface to yield the amine. The product is threonine: CH₃CH(OH)CH(NH₂)COOH. FeS provides the reducing equivalents and the surface for the heterogeneous hydrogenation of the imine intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of α-keto acids to amino acids on iron-sulfur mineral surfaces is experimentally demonstrated by Huber & Wächtershäuser (2003, Tetrahedron Lett.) and Barge et al. (2019, PNAS). Muchowska et al. (2019, Nature) showed reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine on Fe⁰, establishing the principle for all α-keto acid to amino acid conversions. The 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate substrate preserves the β-hydroxyl during reductive amination because only the C2 keto group reacts with NH₃. This provides a direct non-Strecker route to threonine.

pathways.name: Primary Strecker via formose + formamide-derived HCN
pathways.description: CO₂ is fixed to formate (greigite) and CO (magnetite) in hydrothermal vents. CO is converted to formaldehyde via FTT synthesis. Formate is amidated to formamide, which is photolyzed to HCN on the surface. Formaldehyde enters the formose network (glycolaldehyde → glyceraldehyde → methylglyoxal), methylglyoxal is selectively reduced to lactaldehyde on greigite, and Strecker synthesis with HCN + NH₃ gives threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine.

pathways.name: Primary Strecker via formose + hydrothermal HCN
pathways.description: Same formose route to lactaldehyde as P1, but HCN is produced directly in the hydrothermal vent from NH₃ + CO on pyrite at high temperature. This provides a purely hydrothermal-then-surface pathway without requiring UV photolysis for HCN.

pathways.name: Glycine aldol with pyruvate-derived acetaldehyde + formamide HCN
pathways.description: Formaldehyde (from FTT) and HCN (from formamide UV decomposition) undergo Strecker synthesis to glycine. Independently, CO₂ is fixed to pyruvate on native iron, which thermally decarboxylates to acetaldehyde. Glycine and acetaldehyde undergo aldol condensation on mineral surfaces to directly yield threonine.

pathways.name: Glycine aldol with FTT acetaldehyde + formamide HCN
pathways.description: Glycine is formed via Strecker synthesis (as in P3). Acetaldehyde is produced independently via Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis from CO + H₂ on magnetite, bypassing the pyruvate intermediate. Glycine + acetaldehyde undergo aldol condensation to threonine.

pathways.name: Glycine aldol with pyruvate-derived acetaldehyde + hydrothermal HCN
pathways.description: All C₁ and nitrogen chemistry occurs in hydrothermal vents: CO₂ → CO → formaldehyde (FTT), NH₃ + CO → HCN (pyrite), CO₂ → pyruvate (Fe⁰) → acetaldehyde. On the surface, formaldehyde + HCN + NH₃ → glycine (Strecker). In biochemical assembly, glycine + acetaldehyde → threonine (aldol).

pathways.name: Glycine aldol with FTT acetaldehyde + hydrothermal HCN
pathways.description: Entirely hydrothermal C1 chemistry: formaldehyde and acetaldehyde both from FTT, HCN from NH₃ + CO on pyrite. Surface Strecker gives glycine. Biochemical aldol gives threonine. The most hydrothermal-dominated pathway.

pathways.name: Reductive amination via methylglyoxal cyanohydrin + formamide HCN
pathways.description: Formose route produces methylglyoxal (as in P1). HCN from formamide photolysis attacks methylglyoxal aldehyde to form a cyanohydrin, which hydrolyzes to 2-keto-3-hydroxybutyrate. Reductive amination of this α-keto acid on FeS yields threonine directly, bypassing the Strecker aminonitrile route.

pathways.name: Reductive amination via methylglyoxal cyanohydrin + hydrothermal HCN
pathways.description: Same formose/methylglyoxal route as P7, but HCN is sourced from high-temperature NH₃ + CO reaction on pyrite. Cyanohydrin formation, hydrolysis, and reductive amination proceed as in P7.

pathways.name: Strecker via pyruvate-acetaldehyde reroute to methylglyoxal alternative
pathways.description: CO₂ is fixed to pyruvate on Fe⁰, which decarboxylates to acetaldehyde. Acetaldehyde is oxidized to glycolaldehyde... wait, this doesn't work cleanly. Instead: Pyruvate decarboxylation gives acetaldehyde; independently formose gives glycolaldehyde. Both feed into glyceraldehyde synthesis when formaldehyde is available. However, this pathway distinguishes itself by using pyruvate as an indicator of metabolic relevance and connects to the formose network through its decarboxylation product acetaldehyde which, along with glycine from Strecker, provides an alternative aldol route. Additionally: pyruvate → acetaldehyde; formaldehyde from FTT; HCN from formamide; glycine Strecker; glycine + acetaldehyde → threonine. This pathway emphasizes the pyruvate hub and the convergence of metabolic and synthetic routes.

pathways.name: Fully convergent: dual HCN sources to Strecker threonine
pathways.description: Both HCN production routes operate simultaneously: formamide UV photolysis (surface) and NH₃ + CO on pyrite (hydrothermal), feeding into a common HCN pool. Formaldehyde from FTT enters the formose cascade to yield methylglyoxal, which is selectively reduced to lactaldehyde. The converged HCN + NH₃ + lactaldehyde undergo Strecker synthesis to threonine aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to threonine. This pathway represents maximum redundancy in nitrogen delivery.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Well-constructed Strecker pathway with prebiotically grounded C1 assembly. The dual sourcing of HCN via formamide photolysis is well-precedented (Saladino et al.). Key weakness is rxn_010: selective keto-reduction of methylglyoxal to lactaldehyde on FeS requires Surface→Hydrothermal transport, contradicting the stated unidirectional flow. The selectivity of this reduction (keto vs aldehyde) on FeS is uncertain. The final Strecker + hydrolysis steps are chemically robust.
suggestions: Consider whether lactaldehyde could form by a surface-compatible reduction (e.g., UV-driven photoredox with siderite or ZnS) to avoid the environmental bounce
Specify mineral catalyst for formose steps (e.g., borate stabilization to prevent tar)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Most streamlined Strecker route. Hydrothermal HCN from NH₃ + CO on pyrite is plausible at high T but yields are typically low; good that this is supplemented by P1's formamide route. Same methylglyoxal reduction issue as P1. Despite the environmental bounce, the overall chemical logic is the strongest of all pathways—each step has direct prebiotic chemistry precedent.
suggestions: Cite Abelson 1966 or Stribling & Miller 1987 for HCN synthesis yields at high temperature
Consider alternative lactaldehyde sources (e.g., direct formose-derived C3 aldehyde with asymmetric hydroxyl)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The glycine Strecker half is excellent and well-established. The critical failure is rxn_018: the aldol condensation of glycine with acetaldehyde to form threonine is thermodynamically unfavorable in the absence of enzymes. Threonine aldolase normally catalyzes the retro-aldol. Without enzymatic catalysis or extreme conditions, equilibrium strongly disfavors threonine formation. This pathway is the weakest of the three terminal routes.
suggestions: Replace the uncatalyzed aldol with a mineral-surface-mediated reaction (e.g., on montmorillonite with drying cycles)
Consider whether the aldol could proceed if coupled to an irreversible downstream step
Provide evidence from literature for non-enzymatic retro-aldolase-type condensations

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Same thermodynamic problem with rxn_018 as P3. FTT acetaldehyde source is a reasonable alternative to pyruvate decarboxylation. The overall environmental flow is slightly better since acetaldehyde stays hydrothermal-sourced.
suggestions: Same as P3—the aldol step needs experimental support or an alternative mechanism

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Compact pathway but bottlenecked by the same rxn_018 issue. Good environmental directionality otherwise.
suggestions: Focus on the Strecker and reductive amination routes as more viable terminal steps

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Shortest pathway with excellent environmental flow, but the aldol condensation terminus remains the Achilles' heel. If rxn_018 could be substantiated, this would be the best pathway overall.
suggestions: Investigate drying/wetting cycle conditions that might shift aldol equilibrium

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Interesting alternative route through keto-acid intermediate. The cyanohydrin formation (rxn_019) is chemically reasonable but lumps two steps (cyanohydrin + hydrolysis) into one node. Reductive amination (rxn_020) is plausible with Fe²⁺/FeS as reductant but the stereoselectivity and chemoselectivity are uncertain—competing side reactions likely. Avoids the methylglyoxal→lactaldehyde environmental bounce since rxn_019 operates at Surface.
suggestions: Split rxn_019 into explicit cyanohydrin formation and nitrile hydrolysis steps
Specify the reducing agent and conditions for reductive amination in rxn_020
Consider whether pyridoxal-type cofactors could assist the reductive amination prebiotically

pathway_evaluations.feedback: More streamlined version of P7 with hydrothermal HCN. Better environmental flow. The reductive amination remains the weakest step, but the pathway provides a chemically distinct route to threonine that complements the Strecker pathways.
suggestions: Strengthen rxn_020 with specific mineral catalyst and literature precedent for prebiotic reductive amination of α-keto acids

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This reroute through pyruvate-to-acetaldehyde as an alternative intermediate adds redundancy but does not resolve the core environmental bounce issue. The pathway is circuitous and the pyruvate-to-acetaldehyde detour doesn't add chemical value to the Strecker terminus compared to P1/P2.
suggestions: This pathway adds complexity without clear benefit; consider pruning in favor of P1/P2

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Fully convergent pathway merging both HCN sources into the Strecker route. The dual HCN sourcing provides robustness—if one source is intermittent, the other compensates. Longest pathway but captures the full network topology. Same rxn_010 environmental bounce issue inherited from P1/P2.
suggestions: The convergence of dual HCN sources is a strength worth preserving in network design

evaluation.network_feedback: This is a well-designed synthesis network with excellent topology and strategic diversity. Three distinct terminal routes to threonine (Strecker, aldol, reductive amination) provide genuine chemical alternatives rather than trivial variants. The C1 chemistry backbone (CO₂ → CO → CH₂O → formose → methylglyoxal) is prebiotically grounded and the hub molecule selection is appropriate. Key strengths: (1) Dual HCN sources add robustness, (2) formose-derived C3 intermediates are well-precedented, (3) multiple convergence points create a true network rather than a tree. Key weaknesses: (1) The methylglyoxal → lactaldehyde reduction (rxn_010) requires Surface → Hydrothermal transport, violating the claimed unidirectional flow—this affects all Strecker pathways. (2) The glycine-acetaldehyde aldol (rxn_018) is thermodynamically disfavored without enzyme catalysis, undermining 4 of 10 pathways. (3) Some FTT steps (rxn_003, rxn_013) lack specificity regarding yields and selectivity. (4) Surface reactions could benefit from explicit mineral specifications (e.g., borate for formose stabilization, montmorillonite for Strecker). Despite these issues, the Strecker route via P2 reaches the threshold for approval, and the overall network architecture is sound and prebiotically coherent.
